locators.quote: A negotiator's BATNA is the best realistic option available if the current negotiation ends without agreement.
A reservation point is the least favorable deal a negotiator is willing to accept before walking away.
A target point is the realistic outcome a negotiator plans to pursue through offers and concessions.
The zone of possible agreement is the overlap between the terms each side can accept.
Anchoring occurs when an initial serious proposal influences the range of later discussion.
Interests are the underlying needs and concerns that explain why a party takes a stated position.
Integrative bargaining creates value by trading across issues that the parties value differently.
Objective criteria are independent standards, such as market data or precedent, used to evaluate proposals.
A concession is a movement from a prior demand, ideally exchanged for movement by the other side.
A caucus is a private break in which one side or a mediator can reassess options away from the joint table.
A deadline can accelerate decisions, but an artificial deadline may damage trust if exposed.
A package offer presents several connected terms together so trade-offs can be assessed as a whole.
A contingent agreement ties future obligations to an uncertain event that the parties predict differently.
Strategic silence gives the other side room to respond and can prevent unnecessary self-concessions.
An open question invites explanation and cannot usually be answered with only yes or no.
A negotiation summary checks shared understanding by restating agreed points and unresolved issues.
Decision authority is the formal power a representative has to commit their organization to a deal.
Logrolling exchanges concessions on issues each side values differently, allowing mutual gains.
Multiple equivalent simultaneous offers reveal preferences by presenting several packages of equal value to the sender.
An impasse is a point where talks stall because no currently proposed option is acceptable to both sides.
The critical path is the longest chain of dependent project tasks and determines the earliest possible completion date.
Scope creep is the gradual addition of unapproved work without matching adjustments to time, cost, or resources.
A risk register records identified threats, their likelihood and impact, owners, and planned responses.
A retrospective is a recurring team meeting used to inspect recent work and choose process improvements.
A work-in-progress limit caps simultaneous tasks so a team finishes work before starting more.
A dependency exists when one task cannot start or finish until another task reaches a required state.
A milestone marks a significant project event or checkpoint and has no duration of its own.
A stakeholder is a person or group that affects, is affected by, or perceives itself affected by a project.
A project baseline is the approved scope, schedule, or cost plan used as the reference for measuring change.
Change control is the formal process for proposing, assessing, approving, and recording project changes.
Team velocity is the amount of work completed in an iteration and is useful for forecasting that same team's capacity.
Backlog refinement clarifies, splits, estimates, and reorders upcoming work before implementation.
Acceptance criteria are testable conditions that a work item must satisfy to be accepted as complete.
A burn-down chart displays remaining work over time toward the end of an iteration or release.
Resource leveling adjusts task dates to resolve over-allocation of limited people or equipment.
Lead time measures elapsed time from a request being made until the requested value is delivered.
Cycle time measures elapsed time from starting work on an item until that item is finished.
A definition of done is a shared quality checklist applied to every completed increment.
A RACI matrix identifies who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed for each activity.
A timebox is a fixed maximum period allocated to an activity, after which the activity stops or is reviewed.
A BATNA is the action taken if no deal is reached, whereas a reservation point is the worst deal still acceptable.
A target is the desired realistic result, whereas a reservation point is the acceptance boundary.
A position states what a party demands, whereas an interest explains the need motivating that demand.
A mediator facilitates voluntary agreement, whereas an arbitrator issues a decision under the agreed process.
Distributive bargaining allocates a fixed pool, whereas integrative bargaining searches for trades that can expand joint value.
Lead time begins at the request, whereas cycle time begins only when active work starts.
A risk is an uncertain future event, whereas an issue is a problem that has already occurred.
The responsible person performs the work, whereas the accountable person owns the final outcome and approval.
A milestone has zero duration and marks an event, whereas a task consumes time and produces work.
Verification checks whether a product was built to specification, whereas validation checks whether it meets user needs.
Harbor City operates a tidal-energy array as its featured resilience resource.
Program 1 is designed to stabilize winter electricity supply; it stores surplus power for periods of peak demand.
Pine County operates a community seed bank as its featured resilience resource.
Program 2 is designed to protect rare crop varieties; it lends locally adapted seeds to farmers.
Mesa Town operates a solar microgrid as its featured resilience resource.
Program 3 is designed to keep emergency shelters powered; it operates independently during grid outages.
River Borough operates restored wetlands as its featured resilience resource.
Program 4 is designed to reduce downstream storm surges; it holds floodwater before it reaches homes.
Summit Village operates an avalanche sensor network as its featured resilience resource.
Program 5 is designed to shorten avalanche warnings; it sends real-time snow movement alerts.
Cedar District operates a mobile health clinic as its featured resilience resource.
Program 6 is designed to reach remote patients; it rotates primary care through isolated neighborhoods.
Lake Ward operates a rainwater capture system as its featured resilience resource.
Program 7 is designed to reduce potable-water demand; it supplies captured rainwater for irrigation.
Orchard City operates a cold-storage cooperative as its featured resilience resource.
Program 8 is designed to reduce harvest spoilage; it keeps produce cool before shipment.
Delta County operates a floodplain park as its featured resilience resource.
Program 9 is designed to lower insured flood losses; it gives the river safe space during high water.
Granite Town operates a recycled-glass plant as its featured resilience resource.
Program 10 is designed to reduce landfill demand; it turns discarded bottles into paving aggregate.
Willow Borough operates an electric bus depot as its featured resilience resource.
Program 11 is designed to cut transit emissions; it charges buses using overnight renewable power.
Prairie Village operates a soil-testing laboratory as its featured resilience resource.
Program 12 is designed to target fertilizer use; it measures nutrient levels before planting.
Coral District operates a reef nursery as its featured resilience resource.
Program 13 is designed to increase juvenile coral survival; it raises fragments before reef transplantation.
Maple Ward operates a public tool library as its featured resilience resource.
Program 14 is designed to extend the life of household equipment; it allows residents to borrow repair tools.
Dune City operates a desalination pilot as its featured resilience resource.
Program 15 is designed to provide drought backup water; it uses renewable energy to remove salt.
Forest County operates a wildfire watchtower network as its featured resilience resource.
Program 16 is designed to detect fires earlier; it combines cameras with volunteer patrols.
Bay Town operates a fish-market traceability system as its featured resilience resource.
Program 17 is designed to discourage illegal catches; it records each catch from boat to buyer.
Cliff Village operates a cable-car transit link as its featured resilience resource.
Program 18 is designed to connect the ridge without widening roads; it moves passengers above the steep slope.
Meadow Borough operates a pollinator corridor as its featured resilience resource.
Program 19 is designed to support native bee populations; it links flowering habitats across neighborhoods.
Stone District operates a building-heat recovery loop as its featured resilience resource.
Program 20 is designed to lower winter heating demand; it shares recovered waste heat among buildings.
